name: Ki-Nubi (Nubi)

ancestors.notes: Descends from the 19th-century southern-Sudanese pidgin Arabic; sister of Juba Arabic (pga). Carried to Uganda/Kenya by the Emin Pasha Nubi soldiers from the 1890s (Luffin 2005; Wellens 2005).
Sudanese Arabic, closest living relative of the camp lexifier that supplied the bulk of the Ki-Nubi lexicon.
Swahili adstrate in the East African diaspora: the marginal loan phonemes (p, v, tʃ) and much recent vocabulary enter through Swahili and English (Luffin, APiCS ch. 63; Wellens 2005).

notes: Ki-Nubi / Nubi (ISO 639-3: kcn; Glottolog nubi1253) — an Arabic-based creole spoken by the Nubi community of Uganda and Kenya (and diaspora in the DR Congo), the East African offshoot of the same 19th-century southern-Sudanese pidgin that gave rise to Juba Arabic. Carried south by Sudanese ('Nubi') soldiers of Emin Pasha's forces from the 1890s. KEY PHONOLOGY (APiCS chapter 63, Luffin 2013; Wellens 2005): (1) FIVE-VOWEL system i e a o u; vowel LENGTH is not distinctive (rare Kenyan-Nubi minimal pairs aside, e.g. bara/baara). (2) PHARYNGEALS AND EMPHATICS LOST: the Arabic pharyngeals ħ ʕ and the emphatic series tˤ dˤ sˤ ðˤ have no place in the native inventory; the Arabic gutturals q ʔ x ɣ ħ, the interdentals θ ð, and the affricate tʃ survive only marginally in learned Arabic loans and in Swahili/English loans. (3) STRESS is lexically AND grammatically distinctive: sába 'seven' vs. sabá 'morning'; límu 'to gather' vs. limú 'to be gathered' — the passive is marked by a stress shift to the final syllable. ORTHOGRAPHY: no official standard; this spec models the Latin practical orthography used by Luffin and in the APiCS chapter (acute marks the stressed vowel, j = /dʒ/, sh/š = /ʃ/, ny = /ɲ/, ng = /ŋ/, y = /j/). Arabic script is not traditionally used by the community.

sources.author: Luffin, X.
sources.title: Kinubi (Survey chapter and structure dataset)
sources.publisher: In: Michaelis, S.M., Maurer, P., Haspelmath, M. & Huber, M. (eds.), Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures Online, chapter 63, Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology
sources.notes: Open-access, verifiable online. Consonants with marginal (loan-only) segments in parentheses (p, tš, ŋ, x, ġ), five vowels i e a o u, length non-distinctive, stress lexically and grammatically distinctive. Source of the harvested primary-source gold rows.

sources.author: Wellens, I.
sources.title: The Nubi Language of Uganda: An Arabic Creole in Africa
sources.notes: Reference grammar of Ugandan Nubi. Five-vowel system, loss of the Arabic guttural and emphatic series, distinctive stress including the stress-marked passive.

sources.author: Luffin, X.
sources.title: Un créole arabe: le kinubi de Mombasa, Kenya
sources.notes: Description of Kenyan (Mombasa) Nubi; documents the marginal vowel-length pairs and Swahili adstrate.

sources.author: Owens, J.
sources.title: Arabic-based pidgins and creoles
sources.publisher: In: Thomason, S.G. (ed.), Contact Languages: A Wider Perspective, John Benjamins, pp. 125-172
sources.notes: Places Ki-Nubi and Juba Arabic as sister creoles of one southern-Sudanese pidgin.

location.notes: Kampala, Uganda — a principal centre of the Nubi community; the language is also spoken in Kenya and the DR Congo.

stress.notes: Stress is lexically and grammatically distinctive (Luffin, APiCS ch. 63: sába 'seven' vs. sabá 'morning'; límu 'to gather' vs. limú 'to be gathered', the passive marked by a rightward stress shift). Being contrastive it is not weight-predictable; the practical orthography marks it with an acute accent, so a written acute pins the stressed vowel. default_position -2 is only the fallback for unaccented input.
